questions.text: The story of a shirt begins with the production of ________.
questions.explanation: Cotton is the basic raw material needed to manufacture most shirts.

questions.text: Kurnool is a place in ________ famous for cotton cultivation.
questions.explanation: Swapna, a small farmer, lives in Kurnool and grows cotton on her small plot of land.

questions.text: Why do small farmers like Swapna borrow money from local traders?
options: To buy seeds, fertilizers and pesticides
questions.explanation: Agriculture requires expensive inputs, and poor farmers often don't have savings.

questions.text: What condition did the trader put on Swapna for the loan?
options: Sell her cotton only to him
questions.explanation: This ensures the trader gets the cotton at a lower price and can sell it for a profit.

questions.text: Swapna's income from the cotton was ________.
options: Barely more than what she would have earned as a labourer
questions.explanation: After paying back the loan and interest, very little was left for her family's needs.

questions.text: The Erode bi-weekly cloth market is located in ________.
questions.explanation: It is one of the largest cloth markets in the world.

questions.text: What is the 'Putting-out system'?
options: A system where merchants supply raw material and receive finished products from weavers
questions.explanation: In this system, weavers work at home and get paid for their labour by the merchants.

questions.text: A major disadvantage for weavers in the putting-out system is that ________.
options: They don't know the price of the finished cloth and are paid very little
questions.explanation: Merchants have all the power as they own the yarn and control the market.

questions.text: Weavers have to invest ________ of their own money to buy and set up looms.
questions.explanation: A loom can cost up to Rs 20,000, and a weaver family often needs two or more.

questions.text: Weavers' cooperatives are formed so that ________.
options: They can reduce their dependence on merchants and earn more
questions.explanation: Cooperatives help weavers buy yarn and sell cloth directly to the market or government.

questions.text: The garment exporting factory in Delhi mainly sells clothes to ________.
options: Foreign buyers from US and Europe
questions.explanation: These buyers demand high quality and strict delivery schedules at very low prices.

questions.text: To keep their costs low, garment factories ________.
options: Pay the lowest possible wages to workers
questions.explanation: This allows them to maximize their own profits while meeting the demands of foreign buyers.

questions.text: Most workers in garment factories are ________.
questions.explanation: Women are often employed for tasks like thread cutting, buttoning, and ironing.

questions.text: In a US mall, a shirt that was bought from the factory for Rs 300 might be sold for ________.
questions.explanation: The US merchant spends a lot on advertising and storage, and keeps a huge profit for himself.

questions.text: Who earns the *most* in the chain of markets for a shirt?
questions.explanation: The top of the chain (retailers in wealthy countries) takes the largest share of the profit.

questions.text: Who earns the *least* in the chain of markets?
questions.explanation: Those who do the most physical labor (farmers/weavers) often get the smallest financial reward.

questions.text: Markets offer opportunities for work but ________.
options: Do not always provide fair wages
questions.explanation: Equality is often missing in the way profits are distributed in a market chain.

questions.text: A 'Ginning Mill' is a place where ________.
options: Cotton is cleaned and seeds are removed
questions.explanation: This is one of the early steps in the processing of cotton.

questions.text: A 'Merchant' in the Erode market is the one who ________.
options: Links the weaver to the garment exporter
questions.explanation: They take orders from exporters and get the work done through weavers.

questions.text: Cotton needs a ________ to grow.
options: Significant amount of time and chemical inputs (pesticides)
questions.explanation: It is a demanding crop that requires careful management and expensive materials.

questions.text: Why do weavers depend on merchants?
options: For supply of yarn and for selling cloth
questions.explanation: Merchants control the raw materials and the access to buyers.

questions.text: The garment exporter in the story is ________.
options: In the middle between the merchant and the foreign buyer
questions.explanation: He makes more than the weaver but much less than the foreign businessperson.

questions.text: The worker in the Delhi factory who irons the shirts earns about ________ per month.
questions.explanation: Ironing is one of the lower-paid jobs in the factory chain.

questions.text: Democracy is about getting a ________ in the market.
questions.explanation: Whether it's Swapna or the weaver, if they don't get a fair price, they are not equal in the market.

questions.text: A 'Bale' is a ________.
options: Pressed bundle of cotton ready for shipping
questions.explanation: Ginning mills press cotton into bales to send to spinning mills.

questions.text: The 'Powerloom' is ________.
questions.explanation: Electricity-powered looms are much faster than manual ones.

questions.text: Co-optex is a ________.
options: Tamil Nadu government organization that buys cloth from cooperatives
questions.explanation: Government initiatives help ensure weavers get a market for their products.

questions.text: Foreign buyers often have 'Standard' requirements for ________.
options: Quality of production and timely delivery
questions.explanation: Any defect or delay results in heavy penalties for the exporter.

questions.text: The highest paid worker in the garment factory is the ________.
questions.explanation: Tailoring is a skilled job that commands a slightly better wage (around Rs 3,000 in the book's context).

questions.text: Which process turns cotton fibre into yarn?
questions.explanation: Spinning mills take the cotton bales and turn them into threads or yarn.

questions.text: What is the biggest challenge for small farmers like Swapna?
options: Lack of bargaining power against powerful traders
questions.explanation: Because she depends on the trader for loans, she cannot argue about the low price he pays.

questions.text: In Erode, merchants usually give orders based on ________.
options: Demands from garment exporters and retailers
questions.explanation: The merchant is a middleman driven by market demand.

questions.text: A weaver spends almost ________ hours a day on the loom.
questions.explanation: It is a labor-intensive job that requires the whole family to participate.

questions.text: The price of a shirt in a mall includes ________ costs.
questions.explanation: A large part of the final price has nothing to do with the actual manufacturing cost.

questions.text: Does the market give everyone equal benefit?
options: No, it favors the powerful and wealthy
questions.explanation: The wealthy traders and businesspeople earn the most, while producers stay poor.

questions.text: Yarn is used to ________ cloth.
questions.explanation: Yarn consists of spun fibers that are woven on a loom to create fabric.

questions.text: Why do garment exporters accept low prices from foreign buyers?
options: To get large orders and stay in business
questions.explanation: Competition among exporters in different countries (like India, China, Vietnam) keeps prices low.

questions.text: In the putting-out system, the weaver is effectively a ________.
questions.explanation: Although they work at home, they have no control over the product or its price.

questions.text: Which of these is a way to improve the condition of producers?
options: Forming cooperatives and direct market links
questions.explanation: By working together, small producers can gain better bargaining power.

questions.text: The 'Chain of Markets' connects the producer of ________ to the consumer of the ________.
questions.explanation: The story of the shirt illustrates how raw material becomes a finished global product.
